repo_notes.content: CCMath is a header-only C++17 compile-time implementation of the standard <cmath> library. Everything lives under the ccm namespace and is usable in constexpr contexts. Repository: https://github.com/Rinzii/ccmath. License: Apache-2.0 WITH LLVM-exception. Current version: 0.3.0. The library targets 1:1 API compatibility with <cmath> while adding genuine compile-time evaluation, four IEEE rounding modes at runtime, ULP-bounded accuracy, and optional runtime SIMD acceleration. There are no runtime dependencies. The only external libraries (MPFR, GMP, CORE-MATH, Google Benchmark, GoogleTest) are pulled in for testing and benchmarking, never for consumers.

Source layout at a glance. include/ccmath/ holds all 586 headers split into three roots: math/ (134 public API headers, the only headers consumers include), internal/ (423 implementation headers, never part of the public API), and ext/ (28 experimental non-standard extensions). Build glue lives in cmake/ (92 files split into config/, features/, helpers/, modules/, and in/ templates). Tests live in tests/ (129 .cpp files), benchmarks in benchmarks/, fuzz targets in fuzzing/, runnable samples in examples/, and standalone tooling in tools/ (asmlab, coremath, proofs). Status documentation is under docs/ (STATUS.md, ULP_STATUS.md, ROUNDING_STATUS.md).

Three-path dispatch is the central architectural idea. Every nontrivial function chooses among three implementations: (1) a compiler builtin path used only when it is trustworthy (compile time, or runtime under round-to-nearest), (2) a portable generic kernel under internal/math/generic/ that is constexpr-capable and correct in all four rounding modes, and (3) a runtime path under internal/math/runtime/ that may use SIMD or platform tricks. The public header in include/ccmath/math/<family>/<fn>.hpp owns the selection logic. Files suffixed _gen are generic kernels, files suffixed _rt are runtime kernels. Deterministic mode (CCMATH_ENABLE_DETERMINISTIC) forces everything through the generic kernels for bit-identical cross-hardware results.

Build entry points: CMake is primary (3.18+), with Meson and Premake5 provided only for consuming the headers. The interface target is ccmath::ccmath. Configuration presets live in CMakePresets.json. The library is header-only so the target is INTERFACE, and building tests, benchmarks, or fuzzers is opt-in via cache options. CI runs an eight-workflow matrix over Linux, macOS, and Windows and over GCC, Clang, Apple Clang, and MSVC across C++17, C++20, and C++23, plus CodeQL, OpenSSF Scorecard, clang-format lint, and a libFuzzer campaign.

pages.title: Overview
pages.purpose: Introduce CCMath: a header-only, dependency-free C++17 library that reimplements the C++ standard <cmath> so every function works in constexpr (compile-time) contexts, exposed under the ccm namespace. State the core value proposition: genuine compile-time math, 1:1 API parity with <cmath>, IEEE 754 conformance including all four rounding modes at runtime, ULP-bounded accuracy validated against MPFR and CORE-MATH oracles, and optional runtime SIMD acceleration. Summarize the high-level repository map (include/ccmath/{math,internal,ext}, cmake/, tests/, benchmarks/, fuzzing/, examples/, tools/, docs/) and the central three-path dispatch model (builtin vs generic vs runtime). Note licensing (Apache-2.0 WITH LLVM-exception), version 0.3.0, and the upstream repo https://github.com/Rinzii/ccmath. Link to child pages: Getting Started (how to consume) and Library Goals and Design Principles (the rationale). Point readers at README.md for the canonical feature list and docs/STATUS.md for per-function implementation status.

page_notes.content: Source the summary and feature list from README.md (about 11.6 KB). Confirm the namespace is ccm (not ccmath) for functions. The CMake target is ccmath::ccmath. Version 0.3.0 is declared in the root CMakeLists.txt and emitted via cmake/in/version.hpp.in. Any function family that is not yet fully complete should be flagged by pointing to docs/STATUS.md rather than asserting completeness here.

The monolithic include is include/ccmath/ccmath.hpp (a small aggregate, about 44 lines) which pulls in the per-family aggregate headers (basic.hpp, compare.hpp, expo.hpp, fmanip.hpp, hyper.hpp, misc.hpp, nearest.hpp, power.hpp, special.hpp, trig.hpp) plus numbers.hpp. Mention that granular includes are preferred for compile time.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.purpose: Give a consumer the fastest path to using CCMath. Cover three consumption routes: (1) CMake FetchContent pulling https://github.com/Rinzii/ccmath and linking target ccmath::ccmath, (2) vcpkg via the manifest at cmake/vcpkg/vcpkg.json, and (3) plain header inclusion since the library is header-only (add include/ to the include path). Explain the include strategy: the monolithic include/ccmath/ccmath.hpp pulls everything, while granular per-family headers (for example include/ccmath/math/power/sqrt.hpp) cut compile time and are the recommended default. Show a minimal example using ccm:: functions in a constexpr context (for example static_assert(ccm::sqrt(4.0) == 2.0)). Note the minimum toolchain: CMake 3.18+, and a C++17 compiler (GCC 11+, Clang 9+, Apple Clang 14+, MSVC 19.26+). Point to examples/ for runnable programs (constexpr_abs.cpp, hypot_distance.cpp, unit_circle.cpp, fp_classes.cpp, rounding_edge_cases.cpp, and others) and to integration/vendored-consumer/ for a worked external-project setup. Mention that Meson (meson.build) and Premake5 (premake5.lua) are available for consumption but CMake is the supported primary.

page_notes.content: The FetchContent snippet should reference target ccmath::ccmath. Confirm the alias name in the root CMakeLists.txt and cmake/in/ccmath-config.cmake.in before publishing the exact target string. examples/ has matching .run.xml manifests describing expected output. examples/common/io.hpp is shared helper code, and examples/docs/compiler_support.md documents per-compiler caveats.

integration/vendored-consumer/ is a real consumer project used by the ci-integration.yml workflow to prove the install and export work. Use it as the authoritative example of downstream usage rather than inventing CMake snippets.

pages.title: Library Goals and Design Principles
pages.purpose: Explain the non-negotiable principles that shape every line of CCMath. (1) Constexpr-first: every public function must be usable during constant evaluation, and runtime-only optimizations are layered behind is_constant_evaluated checks, never replacing the compile-time path. (2) 1:1 <cmath> API compatibility: signatures, overload sets, special-case behavior (NaN, signed zero, infinities), and errno and fenv semantics match the C++ standard exactly, with no silently wrong values. (3) Accuracy targets: normal accuracy is bounded (the project tracks ULP per function in docs/ULP_STATUS.md), with correctly-rounded results pursued where feasible and validated against MPFR and CORE-MATH oracles. (4) All four IEEE 754 rounding modes work at runtime, not just round-to-nearest, tracked in docs/ROUNDING_STATUS.md. The implementation reads but never sets the rounding mode inside kernels. (5) Zero runtime dependencies and no <cmath> include in the public headers. (6) Cross-platform and cross-compiler portability across the CI matrix. (7) Performance via optional runtime SIMD without compromising the portable path. Reference the examples/ directory and examples/docs/compiler_support.md for the support matrix. Link to the Architecture and Implementation page for how these principles are realized.

page_notes.content: Accuracy and rounding claims must be sourced from docs/STATUS.md, docs/ULP_STATUS.md (about 31 KB), and docs/ROUNDING_STATUS.md (about 18 KB) rather than stated as blanket guarantees. These three files are the living source of truth for what is correctly rounded versus in-contract versus work-in-progress per function and per type. double pow is correctly rounded in all four modes, while powf and ld80 powl are not yet.

The no-fesetround-inside-kernels rule and the no-FENV_ACCESS-pragma rule are firm project constraints. Kernels only read the current rounding mode and adjust bit manipulation and final rounding accordingly. Long double overloads may delegate to double, but double overloads must be real double-width, and float results are never widened to satisfy double.

pages.title: Build System
pages.purpose: Orient the reader on how CCMath is built, tested, and validated. Identify the three build front-ends: CMake (primary, 3.18+), Meson (meson.build, consumption only), and Premake5 (premake5.lua, consumption only). Explain that because the library is header-only, the default build produces only an INTERFACE target, and tests, benchmarks, fuzzers, examples, and oracle suites are each gated behind their own cache option. Map out the cmake/ directory's four functional groups: config/ (project options, install rules, module registries, find-modules for MPFR, GMP, and CORE-MATH), features/ (compile-time detection of builtins and SIMD instruction sets), helpers/ (the ccm_* function helpers that register headers, tests, benchmarks, fuzzers, examples, and oracles), and in/ (configure-time templates for the package config and the generated version.hpp). Summarize the CI/CD surface: eight GitHub Actions workflows covering the platform, compiler, and standard matrix plus security scanning and linting. Point to child pages for CMake configuration detail, the full build-option catalog, and the CI/CD workflows.

page_notes.content: cmake/ contains 92 files. The helper entry points consumers and contributors actually call live in cmake/helpers/: CcmAddHeaders.cmake, CcmTestHelpers.cmake, CcmBenchHelpers.cmake, CcmFuzzHelpers.cmake, CcmExampleHelpers.cmake, CcmOracleHelpers.cmake, and CcmOptionalTooling.cmake. The registries in cmake/config/ (TestModuleRegistry, BenchmarkModuleRegistry, FuzzModuleRegistry, ExampleModuleRegistry, OracleCampaignRegistry) enumerate what gets built.

Meson and Premake5 are explicitly secondary and exist only so downstreams on those build systems can consume the headers. cmake/gen/meson.build is generated. Do not present Meson or Premake as a way to build the test or benchmark suites.

pages.title: CMake Configuration
pages.purpose: Detail how the CMake build is wired. The root CMakeLists.txt (about 7.3 KB) declares project ccmath version 0.3.0, defines the INTERFACE library and the ccmath::ccmath alias, and conditionally includes the test, benchmark, fuzz, example, and oracle subtrees. Explain the header-registration flow: the ccm_add_headers helper (cmake/helpers/CcmAddHeaders.cmake) collects headers into the interface target and drives installation. Cover install and export: cmake/config/InstallRules.cmake plus the templates cmake/in/ccmath-config.cmake.in (the package config consumers find via find_package) and cmake/in/version.hpp.in (configured into a generated version header carrying 0.3.0). Describe ProjectIsTopLevel.cmake, ProjectOptions.cmake, ProjectCompileDefinitions.cmake, and UserOptions.cmake as the layer that decides defaults differently when CCMath is the top-level project versus a subproject. Explain feature detection: cmake/features/ runs compile checks (CheckAllSupportedBuiltinFeatures, CheckAllSupportedSimdFeatures, builtin/support/CheckBuiltinBitCastSupport, CheckBuiltinFmaSupport, simd/SelectSimdInstructionSet, CheckFMASupport, CheckSVMLSupport) and feeds the results into compile definitions. Mention the find-modules FindMpfrGmp.cmake and FindCoreMath.cmake (and cmake/coremath/) used only when oracle or rigorous testing is enabled, and the BuildManifest and EmitBuildManifest pair that records the resolved configuration.

page_notes.content: Read the root CMakeLists.txt to confirm the exact option names and the alias target before documenting them verbatim. The version string flows from the root CMakeLists project() call into cmake/in/version.hpp.in and then into the generated header. Confirm where the generated version.hpp lands (the build directory) so the doc points to the template, not a checked-in artifact.

ProjectIsTopLevel.cmake is the standard guard that suppresses building tests and tools when CCMath is pulled in via FetchContent or add_subdirectory. This is why a downstream FetchContent consumer does not accidentally build the 129-file test suite.

pages.title: Build Options
pages.purpose: Catalog the cache options a builder can toggle, defined primarily in cmake/config/ (ProjectOptions.cmake, UserOptions.cmake, TestValidationOptions.cmake) and consumed across the build. Document each option's name, default, and effect: building the optional subtrees (tests, benchmarks, fuzzers, examples), enabling the experimental extension headers, the deterministic and cross-hardware mode flag (CCMATH_ENABLE_DETERMINISTIC, which forces generic kernels), disabling runtime SIMD, disabling errno semantics, and the warnings-as-errors, aggressive-warnings, and sanitizer toggles used in CI. Explain the rigorous-testing options that turn on MPFR and CORE-MATH oracle campaigns and the worst-case ULP suites (these pull in external dependencies via the find-modules). Note that defaults flip based on top-level versus subproject status so downstreams get a lean header-only consume by default. Direct readers to the CI/CD Workflows page to see which option combinations each workflow exercises.

page_notes.content: Do not publish exact option names or defaults without checking the source. Grep cmake/config/ProjectOptions.cmake, cmake/config/UserOptions.cmake, and cmake/config/TestValidationOptions.cmake for the real CCMATH_* and option() declarations, and check the root CMakeLists.txt for any defined inline. Candidate names such as CCMATH_BUILD_TESTS, CCMATH_DISABLE_RUNTIME_SIMD, CCMATH_ENABLE_EXTENSIONS, and CCMATH_DISABLE_ERRNO are unverified. Confirm each against the source before stating it as fact.

Werror builds are real: there is a build-werror/ output directory and CI builds with warnings-as-errors plus aggressive warnings (-Wconversion and related). Deterministic mode is CCMATH_ENABLE_DETERMINISTIC and routes through generic kernels, so accuracy in that mode is capped by the generic implementations.

pages.title: CI/CD Workflows
pages.purpose: Explain the continuous-integration surface under .github/workflows/. Document the eight workflows: ci-linux.yml (GCC and Clang across C++17, C++20, and C++23), ci-macos.yml (Apple Clang), ci-windows.yml (MSVC), ci-integration.yml (builds the integration/vendored-consumer/ project to prove install and export work for downstreams), fuzz.yml (runs the libFuzzer campaign and smoke targets), codeql.yml (CodeQL static analysis), scorecard.yml (OpenSSF Scorecard supply-chain posture), and lint.yml (clang-format enforcement). State the compiler and standard matrix: Linux GCC 11+ and Clang 9+, macOS Apple Clang 14+, Windows MSVC 19.26+, each across C++17, C++20, and C++23. Describe how the workflows reuse the CMake build options to enable tests and the strict-warnings build, and how the fuzz workflow ties into fuzzing/cmake/run_fuzz_campaign.cmake and run_fuzz_smoke.cmake. Mention the pre-commit configuration (.pre-commit-config.yaml) and the lint.sh and lint.bat scripts contributors run locally to match lint.yml. Note that Dependabot was removed, so dependency updates are manual. Security scanning is covered by CodeQL and Scorecard.

page_notes.content: Open each file in .github/workflows/ to confirm exact job names, the compiler version floors, and which standards each matrix leg actually runs before stating them. The eight filenames are confirmed: ci-linux.yml, ci-macos.yml, ci-windows.yml, ci-integration.yml, fuzz.yml, codeql.yml, lint.yml, scorecard.yml.

Dependabot was intentionally removed (see commit 'Remove dependabot configuration (#145)'). Do not document Dependabot as active. Clang-format is pinned, and the repo runs a clang-format check on every pull request (recent commit '575fd55f Run clang-format check on every pull request').

pages.title: Core Components
pages.purpose: Provide the index of CCMath's public mathematical API, all under include/ccmath/math/ and surfaced in the ccm namespace. Enumerate the ten function families, each with its own subdirectory: basic/ (abs, fdim, fma, fmod, fmax, fmin, nan, remainder, remquo), compare/ (fpclassify, isfinite, isinf, isnan, isnormal, signbit, isgreater, isgreaterequal, isless, islessequal, islessgreater, isunordered), expo/ (exp, exp2, expm1, log, log2, log10, log1p), fmanip/ (copysign, frexp, ilogb, ldexp, logb, modf, nextafter, nexttoward, scalbn), hyper/ (sinh, cosh, tanh, asinh, acosh, atanh), misc/ (gamma, lgamma, lerp), nearest/ (ceil, floor, round, rint, nearbyint, trunc), power/ (sqrt, cbrt, hypot, pow), special/ (the C++17 special mathematical functions: Bessel, elliptic integrals, Legendre, Laguerre, Hermite, beta, Riemann zeta, and the exponential integral, 21 headers), and trig/ (sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, atan2). Explain the consistent file pattern: include/ccmath/math/<family>/<fn>.hpp is the public entry that performs three-path dispatch into internal/math/generic/func/<family>/ and internal/math/runtime/func/<family>/. Note the per-family aggregate headers (basic.hpp, compare.hpp, special.hpp, and the rest), with misc/ as the exception: it has no aggregate header, and include/ccmath/ccmath.hpp pulls in gamma.hpp, lgamma.hpp, and lerp.hpp individually. Mention the master include/ccmath/ccmath.hpp and numbers.hpp for constants. Direct readers to the per-family child pages and to docs/STATUS.md for the authoritative completion and accuracy state of each function.

page_notes.content: Header counts: math/ has 134 public headers total across basic/ (11), compare/ (14), expo/ (10), fmanip/ (14), hyper/ (6), misc/ (3), nearest/ (8), power/ (4), special/ (21), and trig/ (10). The special/ family is the largest and implements the C++17 special-math set (IS 29124), not plain <cmath>. erf and erfc are not exposed as public functions, and only internal builtin wrappers exist under internal/math/generic/builtins/misc/. Accuracy here is the least mature in the library, so confirm which functions are in-contract via docs/STATUS.md and docs/ULP_STATUS.md before describing them as complete.

numbers.hpp provides mathematical constants analogous to the C++20 <numbers> header but constexpr-clean for C++17. The public headers must not include <cmath>. Verify with the conformance include-policy test (tests/conformance/include_policy_test.cpp).

pages.title: Basic Math Functions
pages.purpose: Document the basic family in include/ccmath/math/basic/ (11 public headers): abs, fdim, fma, fmod, fmax, fmin, nan, remainder, and remquo. For each, describe the standard semantics it mirrors, the special-case handling (NaN propagation, signed zero, infinities), and how the public header dispatches across the three paths into internal/math/generic/func/basic/ (constexpr-capable kernels) and internal/math/runtime/func/basic/ (runtime and SIMD paths). Give fma special attention: it must be a true fused multiply-add (single rounding), is correctly rounded, and is validated by dedicated conformance tests (tests/conformance/fma_rounding_test.cpp, fma_software_fenv_test.cpp) and MPFR oracles (tests/shared/oracle/mpfr_fma_double.cpp, mpfr_fma_float.cpp). Explain remquo's quotient-bits output and remainder's IEEE remainder definition. Note that the basic family is also where the runtime SIMD story is most visible (abs, fdim, and fma have benchmarks under benchmarks/src/math/basic/).

page_notes.content: fma was already correctly rounded before the pow correct-rounding work. It relies on internal/support/fp/fma.hpp and the EFT (error-free transform) ops in internal/support/fp/ops/. The software-fenv FMA test exists because some platforms lack a hardware FMA and the generic path must still honor the rounding mode.

Benchmarks exist for abs, fdim, and fma in benchmarks/src/math/basic/. Unit tests are in tests/src/math/basic/ (abs, fdim, fma, fmod, max, min, nan, remainder, remquo). Confirm the exact 11 header filenames in include/ccmath/math/basic/ before listing them.

pages.title: Comparison Functions
pages.purpose: Document the compare family in include/ccmath/math/compare/ (14 public headers): the classification predicates fpclassify, isfinite, isinf, isnan, isnormal, and signbit, plus the relational predicates isgreater, isgreaterequal, isless, islessequal, islessgreater, and isunordered. Explain that these are the functions where compiler builtins are most often trustworthy even in constexpr, so the dispatch frequently prefers __builtin_* paths gated by CCM_HAS_CONST_BUILTIN, falling back to bit-level generic kernels (for example fpclassify uses the FPBits representation and a helper at internal/support/helpers/fpclassify_helper.hpp). Cover the critical correctness details: signbit must report the sign bit of negative zero and NaN correctly, the relational predicates must not raise the invalid exception for quiet NaNs the way a naive less-than or greater-than would, and isunordered defines the NaN-aware ordering. Point to internal/math/generic/func/compare/ for the portable kernels and tests/src/math/compare/ for the unit tests.

page_notes.content: fpclassify returns the standard FP_* categories and is backed by internal/support/helpers/fpclassify_helper.hpp and FPBits. signbit correctness on -0.0 and NaN is a common failure point. The trig overhaul work fixed sign-of-zero issues elsewhere, so cross-link to FPBits handling.

There are 14 headers here including the six relational predicates and the classification ones. Unit tests in tests/src/math/compare/ include fpclassify, isfinite, isgreater variants, isinf, isless variants, isnan, isnormal, and signbit. Verify the full filename set in the directory.

pages.title: Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
pages.purpose: Document the expo family in include/ccmath/math/expo/ (10 public headers): exp, exp2, expm1, log, log2, log10, and log1p, each with float, double, and long-double overloads. Explain the implementation strategy: argument reduction plus polynomial and minimax approximation evaluated in the generic kernels under internal/math/generic/func/expo/, with constants chosen and validated through the Sollya and Gappa workflow documented in docs/approximating_functions/. Describe the accuracy posture (ULP bounds tracked in docs/ULP_STATUS.md) and the special-case requirements (exp of positive or negative infinity, log of zero, negative, or NaN, and expm1 and log1p near zero for catastrophic-cancellation avoidance). Note the dedicated ULP-accuracy unit tests in tests/src/math/expo/ and that these functions lean on shared helpers (internal/support/helpers/exp_helpers.hpp, exp10.hpp, poly_eval.hpp, multiply_add.hpp). Cross-link to Floating-Point Representation for how FPBits and DyadicFloat support the high-precision intermediate steps.

page_notes.content: expm1 and log1p exist precisely for the near-zero accuracy case and must not be implemented as exp(x) minus 1 or log(1 plus x). Polynomial constants should be demystified and reproducible via the Sollya scripts under docs/approximating_functions/. The proof-freshness check (tests/proofs/proof_freshness_check.py) guards that the committed constants still match their derivation.

Helpers used: internal/support/poly_eval.hpp (Horner and Estrin evaluation), internal/support/multiply_add.hpp, internal/support/helpers/exp_helpers.hpp, and exp10.hpp. Unit tests in tests/src/math/expo/ include exp, exp2, expm1, log, log10, log1p, log2, and a ULP-accuracy test.

pages.title: Power Functions
pages.purpose: Document the power family in include/ccmath/math/power/ (4 public headers): sqrt, cbrt, hypot, and pow. Sqrt shows the dispatch pattern in its simplest form: the public header selects among a correctly-rounded hardware or builtin path, a generic sqrt_gen fallback (Newton or Goldschmidt style with a final correctly-rounded step), and a runtime path. Other nontrivial functions follow the same shape. Pow is the most complex and most heavily validated function in the library: the double overload is correctly rounded in all four IEEE rounding modes (achieved by fixing four classes of bugs), implemented via a log and exp decomposition with extended-precision (DyadicFloat) intermediates under internal/math/generic/func/power/pow_impl/, and exercised by oracle campaigns against MPFR and CORE-MATH plus Gappa proofs. Hypot must avoid intermediate overflow and underflow and be correctly rounded. Describe the runtime SIMD pow paths, the generic constexpr paths, and the deterministic-mode behavior. Direct readers to docs/STATUS.md, docs/ULP_STATUS.md, docs/ROUNDING_STATUS.md, tests/proofs/math/power/, tests/rigorous/, and the conformance pow tests for the authoritative correctness picture.

page_notes.content: Accuracy facts: double pow is correctly rounded in all four modes after fixing four bug classes, while powf (float) and ld80 powl (80-bit long double) are not yet correctly rounded and are left as-is. fma was already correctly rounded. Do not claim float or long-double pow correctness. The current branch is fix/fuzz-guard-ld80-powl-near-one, so ld80 powl near 1.0 is actively being hardened.

Pow testing has a trap worth calling out: some test campaigns measure the system libm via __builtin_pow rather than ccmath's kernel (the public_default and simple campaigns hit libm, while the generic_runtime campaign hits the ccmath kernel). MSVC UCRT std::pow ignores the rounding mode at range thresholds, so never use the system libm as a directed-rounding oracle. Use MPFR or exact IEEE values. See tests/conformance/pow_path_config_test.cpp and pow_errno_fenv_test.cpp, tests/proofs/math/power/ (pow_double_proof.py, the Gappa .g files), and tests/rigorous/oracle_logs/.

pow_impl/ under internal/math/generic/func/power/ holds the layered Ziv-style implementation. Unit tests in tests/src/math/power/ are extensive (19 files): pow correct-rounding, deterministic, and boundary variants, plus sqrt, hypot, cbrt, a SIMD test, and accuracy tests. The sqrt benchmark is at benchmarks/src/math/power/sqrt.bench.cpp.

pages.title: Nearest Integer Functions
pages.purpose: Document the nearest family in include/ccmath/math/nearest/ (8 public headers): ceil, floor, trunc, round, rint, and nearbyint. Explain the shared mechanism: these are implemented by direct manipulation of the floating-point representation via FPBits rather than arithmetic, masking off the fractional mantissa bits according to the unbiased exponent (see the truncation and nearest-integer logic in internal/support/fp/nearest_integer.hpp and directional_rounding_utils.hpp). Clarify the semantic differences that matter: trunc rounds toward zero, floor toward negative infinity, ceil toward positive infinity, round is round-half-away-from-zero (and is not affected by the current rounding mode), rint rounds using the current rounding mode and may raise the inexact exception, and nearbyint behaves like rint but never raises inexact. Describe the constexpr versus runtime dispatch and that these functions must preserve the sign of zero and pass NaN and infinity through unchanged. Point to tests/src/math/nearest/ and the rounding conformance tests.

page_notes.content: The rint-versus-nearbyint distinction (the inexact exception) and round being mode-independent are the two most common correctness traps, both covered by tests/conformance/rounding_conformance_test.cpp and ulp_all_modes_test.cpp. The bit manipulation lives in internal/support/fp/nearest_integer.hpp. Cross-link to Floating-Point Representation.

trunc has a benchmark at benchmarks/src/math/nearest/trunc.bench.cpp. Unit tests in tests/src/math/nearest/ cover ceil, floor, nearbyint, round, rint, and trunc. Confirm the 8 header filenames in the directory (six functions plus possibly aggregate or forwarding headers).

pages.title: Floating-Point Manipulation
pages.purpose: Document the fmanip family in include/ccmath/math/fmanip/ (14 public headers): copysign, frexp, ldexp, modf, scalbn (and scalbln), logb, ilogb, nextafter, and nexttoward. Explain that these functions decompose or recompose the floating-point representation, so they are implemented almost entirely through FPBits and exponent and mantissa manipulation in internal/math/generic/func/fmanip/ with helpers like internal/support/helpers/internal_ldexp.hpp. Cover the per-function contracts: copysign composes magnitude with sign bit (must work for NaN and zero), frexp splits into normalized fraction and exponent, ldexp and scalbn scale by a power of two efficiently and correctly across the subnormal range with proper overflow, underflow, errno, and fenv, modf splits integral and fractional parts preserving sign, logb and ilogb extract the unbiased exponent (with the special ilogb return values FP_ILOGB0 and FP_ILOGBNAN), and nextafter and nexttoward step to the adjacent representable value (raising overflow, underflow, or inexact as required). Note the long-double specifics for nexttoward and the type-precision rule that double overloads are real double-width. Point to tests/src/math/fmanip/.

page_notes.content: scalbn and ldexp must traverse subnormals correctly and set errno or raise fenv on overflow and underflow, a frequent bug source. ilogb has the special returns FP_ILOGB0 and FP_ILOGBNAN. nextafter and nexttoward differ in the type of the second argument (long double for nexttoward) and in the exact representable-neighbor stepping.

There are 14 headers and unit tests in tests/src/math/fmanip/ for copysign, frexp, ilogb, ldexp, logb, modf, nextafter, nexttoward, and scalbn. Confirm whether scalbln has its own header. Helper: internal/support/helpers/internal_ldexp.hpp.

pages.title: Trigonometric and Hyperbolic Functions
pages.purpose: Document two families. Trigonometric: include/ccmath/math/trig/ (10 public headers) with sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, and atan2. Hyperbolic: include/ccmath/math/hyper/ with sinh, cosh, tanh, asinh, acosh, and atanh. Explain the trig implementation overhaul: double sin, cos, and tan are in-contract across all argument ranges using real binary64 constants plus Payne-Hanek reduction for large arguments (so accuracy does not collapse far from zero), and the sign-of-zero and inverse-trig edge cases were fixed and committed. Describe the dispatch into internal/math/generic/func/trig/ and internal/math/generic/func/hyper/, the use of poly_eval and multiply_add helpers, and the special cases (sin or cos of infinity is NaN, atan2 quadrant and signed-zero rules, asin and acos domain of negative one to one, acosh domain of one or greater, atanh domain between negative one and one). Note that builtin dispatch headers exist but the generic kernels hold the actual implementation. Point to tests/src/math/trig/ (including a kernel-regression test and a ULP-accuracy test) and docs/ULP_STATUS.md.

page_notes.content: Trig overhaul facts (committed): double sin, cos, and tan use real binary64 constants and Payne-Hanek large-argument reduction, with sign-of-zero and inverse-trig edge cases fixed. The large-argument reduction is the key to staying in-contract for huge inputs. Cross-link to Extended Precision Types since the reduction needs high-precision pi multiples. There is a kernel-regression test in tests/src/math/trig/ guarding against re-introducing the fixed bugs.

Confirm the exact header set: trig/ has 10 headers (sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, atan2, plus possibly forwarding or builtin headers). The hyper/ count is not pinned, so list sinh, cosh, tanh, asinh, acosh, and atanh and verify. Generic kernels are at internal/math/generic/func/trig/ and internal/math/generic/func/hyper/.

pages.title: Special Functions
pages.purpose: Document the special family in include/ccmath/math/special/ (21 public headers), CCMath's implementation of the C++17 special mathematical functions (IS 29124). Group them by kind: cylindrical and spherical Bessel functions (cyl_bessel_i, cyl_bessel_j, cyl_bessel_k, cyl_neumann, sph_bessel, sph_neumann), Legendre and associated and spherical Legendre (legendre, assoc_legendre, sph_legendre), Laguerre and associated Laguerre (laguerre, assoc_laguerre), Hermite polynomials (hermite), complete and incomplete elliptic integrals of the first, second, and third kinds (ellint_1, ellint_2, ellint_3, comp_ellint_1, comp_ellint_2, comp_ellint_3), the beta function (beta), the Riemann zeta function (riemann_zeta), and the exponential integral (expint). State plainly that erf and erfc are not part of this public set, since only internal builtin wrappers for them exist under internal/math/generic/builtins/misc/. Explain that each public header dispatches into a generic kernel under internal/math/generic/func/special/ (the matching _gen files), that the family is aggregated by include/ccmath/math/special.hpp, and that accuracy here is the least mature in the library. The per-function status in docs/STATUS.md and docs/ULP_STATUS.md is authoritative. Read the impl headers and status docs to state domains, recurrences, and accuracy rather than assuming them, and stress MPFR oracle validation for these higher-complexity routines.

page_notes.content: Header set (21, all listed in special.hpp): assoc_laguerre, assoc_legendre, beta, comp_ellint_1, comp_ellint_2, comp_ellint_3, cyl_bessel_i, cyl_bessel_j, cyl_bessel_k, cyl_neumann, ellint_1, ellint_2, ellint_3, expint, hermite, laguerre, legendre, riemann_zeta, sph_bessel, sph_legendre, sph_neumann. The generic kernels are the matching _gen files under internal/math/generic/func/special/.

This is the C++17 special-math set (IS 29124), not part of plain <cmath>, so frame it that way for readers. Accuracy and completeness vary by function, so do not assert correctness or ULP bounds without checking docs/STATUS.md and docs/ULP_STATUS.md. Given their complexity, these functions are the strongest candidates for MPFR oracle validation.

pages.title: Miscellaneous Functions
pages.purpose: Document the misc family in include/ccmath/math/misc/: gamma (the true gamma function, matching tgamma), lgamma (the natural log of the absolute value of gamma), and lerp (linear interpolation, matching the C++20 std::lerp). Unlike the other families, misc/ has no aggregate header, so include/ccmath/ccmath.hpp includes gamma.hpp, lgamma.hpp, and lerp.hpp individually under a comment that labels them uncategorized. Explain the implementations: gamma and lgamma have type-specific kernels and a shared data table under include/ccmath/math/misc/impl/ (gamma_data.hpp, gamma_double_impl.hpp, gamma_float_impl.hpp, gamma_impl.hpp, lgamma_double_impl.hpp, lgamma_impl.hpp), and the writeup should read those headers to state the actual approximation method, the reflection handling for negative arguments, and the pole behavior at non-positive integers rather than assuming a specific algorithm. Cover lerp's standard guarantees: it returns a at t equal to zero, b at t equal to one, and is monotonic. Point to tests/src/math/misc/ for the gamma, lgamma, and lerp tests, including the std versus no_std comparison variants.

page_notes.content: misc/ holds gamma, lgamma, and lerp. There is no misc.hpp aggregate, and the three headers are included individually by include/ccmath/ccmath.hpp under an uncategorized comment. The gamma and lgamma kernels live under include/ccmath/math/misc/impl/ with separate float and double implementations plus a shared gamma_data.hpp table.

Confirm gamma maps to tgamma semantics by reading include/ccmath/math/misc/gamma.hpp, and read the impl headers before naming the approximation method. lerp matches C++20 std::lerp, so verify the endpoint exactness and the handling of out-of-range t. Tests in tests/src/math/misc/ include std and no_std variants.

pages.title: Extension Functions
pages.purpose: Document the non-standard extension library under include/ccmath/ext/ (28 headers), gated as experimental with an explicitly unstable API (see include/ccmath/ext/README.md). These are convenience and graphics or gameplay-oriented helpers not found in <cmath>: clamp, saturate, lerp helpers (mix, lerp_smooth, inverse_lerp and unlerp, remap), angle helpers (degrees, radians, delta_angle, lerp_angle, move_towards, move_towards_angle), interpolation and easing (smoothstep, cubic, step), periodic helpers (ping_pong, repeat, fract), and assorted utilities (abs_diff, align, approximately, ceil_div, chgsign, normalize, rcp, sign, is_power_of_two). Explain that, like the core API, they are constexpr-capable and live under the ccm namespace (typically ccm::ext or ccm:: per the headers), and that enabling them may be controlled by a build option. Stress their experimental status: signatures and behavior may change, and they are not held to the same <cmath>-conformance bar as the core families. Point to tests/src/math/ext/ (28 matching test files plus a constexpr smoke test) for usage examples.

page_notes.content: Full 28-header list: abs_diff, align, approximately, ceil_div, chgsign, clamp, cubic, degrees, delta_angle, fract, inverse_lerp, is_power_of_two, lerp_angle, lerp_smooth, mix, move_towards, move_towards_angle, normalize, ping_pong, radians, rcp, remap, repeat, saturate, sign, smoothstep, step, unlerp. The README at include/ccmath/ext/README.md states the stability policy. Quote it rather than paraphrasing the guarantee.

Verify the exact namespace these land in (ccm:: versus ccm::ext) by reading one header such as include/ccmath/ext/clamp.hpp. Each extension has a 1:1 unit test in tests/src/math/ext/ plus a constexpr_smoke_test.cpp confirming constant-evaluation usability.

pages.title: Architecture and Implementation
pages.purpose: Give the internal-architecture overview that ties the implementation pages together. Center it on the three-path dispatch model: each public header in include/ccmath/math/ selects among (1) a compiler-builtin path (trusted only at compile time or under round-to-nearest at runtime), (2) a portable generic kernel under include/ccmath/internal/math/generic/ that is constexpr-capable and correct in all four rounding modes (files often suffixed _gen), and (3) a runtime kernel under include/ccmath/internal/math/runtime/ that may use SIMD or platform tricks (files often suffixed _rt). Explain the supporting subsystems housed under include/ccmath/internal/: predef/ and config/ for compiler, platform, and builtin detection, support/ for the shared numeric machinery (FPBits, fenv, EFT ops, helpers, type traits, extended-precision types), and types/ for internal type definitions. Describe how is_constant_evaluated gates compile-time versus runtime selection and how deterministic mode forces the generic path for bit-identical results. Link to the child pages: Type System, Compiler Support and Builtins, SIMD Optimization, Floating-Point Representation, and Extended Precision Types.

page_notes.content: Naming conventions to verify and document: kernels under internal/math/generic/func/<family>/ versus internal/math/runtime/func/<family>/, the _gen and _rt suffixes, and builtin wrappers under internal/math/generic/builtins/. The dispatch decision lives in the public header, not in a separate dispatch layer, and the project explicitly forbids adding new parallel layers. Cross-link to the sqrt pattern as the canonical dispatch reference.

internal/ holds 423 of the 586 headers. The rule for contributors: constexpr only goes as deep as the lowest primitive needs it, and every non-generic (runtime) path in a _rt.hpp file must guard against the current rounding mode. is_constant_evaluated lives at internal/support/is_constant_evaluated.hpp.

pages.title: Type System
pages.purpose: Describe CCMath's internal type infrastructure under include/ccmath/internal/support/ and include/ccmath/internal/types/. Cover the type-trait and type-support layer: type_traits.hpp (constexpr-friendly traits), limits.hpp (a constexpr std::numeric_limits stand-in), is_constant_evaluated.hpp (the C++17 and C++20 portable wrapper), floating_point_traits.hpp, meta_compare.hpp, integer_literals.hpp, and the always_false<T> idiom for dependent static_assert in templates. Explain the small domain types that model numeric concepts precisely: a Sign type (positive or negative sign abstraction), a NumberPair or pair-like structure for returning two related values (for example the high and low parts of an EFT), and a NormalizedFloat type used in reductions. Note that these types exist so the kernels can be written generically over float, double, and long double while staying constexpr and dependency-free (no <type_traits> pulled into the public surface where it can be avoided). Cross-link to Floating-Point Representation (FPBits) and Extended Precision Types (BigInt, DyadicFloat), which build on this layer. Point to tests/src/math/internal/ for the type unit tests (big_int, dyadic_float, normalized_float).

page_notes.content: Confirm the exact names and locations of Sign, NumberPair, and NormalizedFloat by grepping include/ccmath/internal/ (likely under internal/types/ or internal/support/). These type names are unverified, so confirm they exist as named types before documenting their APIs. NormalizedFloat has a dedicated test (tests/src/math/internal/types/), so it is real.

limits.hpp and is_constant_evaluated.hpp are the portability backbone for C++17 where std::is_constant_evaluated is C++20. always_false is the standard trick for making static_assert fire only on instantiation. type_traits.hpp re-implements the subset of <type_traits> the kernels need.

pages.title: Compiler Support and Builtins
pages.purpose: Explain how CCMath detects and uses compiler builtins and how it falls back when they are absent or untrustworthy. Cover the detection macros: CCM_HAS_BUILTIN (is the builtin available) and CCM_HAS_CONST_BUILTIN (is it usable in a constant expression), implemented in include/ccmath/internal/predef/has_builtin.hpp and has_const_builtin.hpp on top of has_attribute.hpp. Describe the builtin-support headers under internal/math/generic/builtins/ that wrap __builtin_* math functions behind a uniform interface, and the compile-time feature checks mirrored in cmake/features/builtin/support/. Cover the predef attribute layer (include/ccmath/internal/predef/attributes/ and related): always_inline, the likely and unlikely branch hints (likely.hpp, unlikely.hpp), assume.hpp (assumption hint), and optnone and optimization controls, plus the compiler-detection and versioning headers (internal/predef/versioning/) that identify GCC, Clang, MSVC, and Apple Clang and their versions. Explain the policy: builtins are trusted only at compile time or under FE_TONEAREST, and every non-generic runtime path that uses a builtin must guard for the active rounding mode. Cross-link to SIMD Optimization (which has its own feature-detection layer) and to Library Goals (the trust-builtins rule).

page_notes.content: The builtin-trust rule is a core numeric-correctness invariant: __builtin_* math functions are trusted only at compile time or under round-to-nearest. This is why the three-path model exists. internal/predef/ holds has_builtin.hpp, has_const_builtin.hpp, has_attribute.hpp, assume.hpp, likely.hpp, and unlikely.hpp, with subdirectories attributes/, compiler_suppression/, and versioning/.

Verify the exact macro spellings (CCM_HAS_BUILTIN, CCM_HAS_CONST_BUILTIN) and the attribute header filenames by reading internal/predef/. The compiler-detection header name is unverified (a likely candidate had a typo such as 'compiler_suppport_detection.hpp'), so confirm the real filename before linking it.

pages.title: SIMD Optimization
pages.purpose: Detail the runtime SIMD subsystem under include/ccmath/internal/math/runtime/simd/. Explain the two-stage story: compile-time instruction-set selection (driven by cmake/features/simd/SelectSimdInstructionSet.cmake, CheckFMASupport.cmake, CheckSVMLSupport.cmake) and header-level feature detection (a check_simd_support-style header exposing macros for the available ISA). Describe the abstraction: a native-ABI dispatcher (the ccm::intrin abi::native concept) and a basic_simd<T, Abi> vector type that the runtime kernels program against, with per-ISA backends under runtime/simd/func/impl/: scalar/ (portable fallback), sse2/, sse4/, avx2/, neon/ (ARM), and vector_size/ (the GCC and Clang generic vector extension). Explain that SIMD is strictly a runtime acceleration: it is never used during constant evaluation and is disabled in deterministic mode and when the disable-runtime-SIMD build option is set, with the scalar backend always available as the fallback. Note the instruction-abstraction headers under runtime/simd/instructions/. Mention the parallel ccm::pp effort (a C++17 port of C++26 std::simd under runtime/pp/) that is standalone and not yet wired into the kernels. Point to tests/src/math/internal/ (the SIMD test) and the abs and sqrt SIMD-path benchmarks.

page_notes.content: Six backends are present: scalar, sse2, sse4, avx2, neon, and vector_size under internal/math/runtime/simd/func/impl/. There is both a live ccm::intrin SIMD layer and a newer standalone ccm::pp port of C++26 std::simd under runtime/pp/ that is not yet wired in. Document ccm::intrin as the active path and ccm::pp as forthcoming and experimental.

Verify the exact names basic_simd, abi::native, and the check_simd_support header by reading internal/math/runtime/simd/. CMake side: cmake/features/CheckAllSupportedSimdFeatures.cmake, simd/SelectSimdInstructionSet.cmake, CheckFMASupport.cmake, and CheckSVMLSupport.cmake (SVML is the Intel short vector math library). Disabling SIMD is a build option (verify its real name) and deterministic mode bypasses SIMD entirely.

pages.title: Floating-Point Representation
pages.purpose: Explain how CCMath inspects and constructs floating-point values bit-for-bit, the foundation under nearly every kernel. Center on FPBits and the FPRepImpl hierarchy in include/ccmath/internal/support/fp/fp_bits.hpp: a type that decomposes an IEEE value into sign, exponent, and mantissa with constexpr accessors, parameterized over the format. Describe FPType and FPLayout for the four IEEE formats binary32, binary64, binary80 (x87 extended, with its explicit integer bit), and binary128, and the BiasedExponent versus Exponent distinction (stored-with-bias versus unbiased). Cover the EFT and rounding machinery: the error-free-transform operations under internal/support/fp/ops/ (add, sub, mul, add_or_sub) used for two-sum and two-product, the correctly-rounded helpers in internal/support/fp/ (fma.hpp, cast.hpp, nearest_integer.hpp), and the directional rounding utilities (directional_rounding_utils.hpp) that let kernels emit correctly-rounded results in any mode without changing the FPU mode. Describe the fenv abstraction under internal/support/fenv/ (reading the current rounding mode, raising exceptions) and the errno handling, both honoring <cmath> semantics while remaining constexpr-friendly. Cross-link to Extended Precision Types and to the rounding and conformance tests (tests/conformance/).

page_notes.content: FPBits is the core primitive. The project review standard says to reuse FPBits and the EFT ops rather than reinventing bit manipulation. internal/support/fp/ contains fp_bits.hpp, fma.hpp, cast.hpp, directional_rounding_utils.hpp, nearest_integer.hpp, and ops/ (add, sub, mul, add_or_sub). binary80 (long double on x86) has an explicit integer bit that makes it special-case-heavy, which is why ld80 powl is still being hardened.

Firm rules to state: kernels never call fesetround and only read the rounding mode to adjust bit manipulation and final rounding. The #pragma STDC FENV_ACCESS ON pragma is forbidden (unportable and widely ignored). The fenv layer is at internal/support/fenv/. errno gating exists but set_errno_if_required is not constexpr (a known gap, deliberately left unfixed), so compile-time error paths are ill-formed.

pages.title: Extended Precision Types
pages.purpose: Document the multiword and extended-precision numeric types under include/ccmath/internal/support/ (and types/) that the hard functions rely on for accurate intermediates. Cover BigInt<Bits, Signed, WordType>: a constexpr fixed-width big-integer built from an array of machine words, supporting the full arithmetic, shift, and comparison set, used where 64-bit (or 128-bit) integers are insufficient (for example Payne-Hanek trig reduction and pow's mantissa work). Cover DyadicFloat<Bits>: a high-precision floating-point type (a sign plus a BigInt mantissa plus an exponent) used as the extended-precision intermediate in pow, exp and log, and the trig reductions to push results inside correctly-rounded tolerance before the final round-back to the target type. Cover the fixed-width integer wrappers float128 and int128 and uint128 (with portable fallbacks where the compiler lacks __int128 or __float128), and the bit-counting support utilities ctz.hpp (count trailing zeros) and popcount used by the big-integer routines (internal/support/ctz.hpp, bits.hpp). Explain that these are all constexpr and dependency-free so they work during constant evaluation. Point to tests/src/math/internal/types/ for the big_int, dyadic_float, and normalized_float unit tests.

page_notes.content: BigInt and DyadicFloat both have dedicated unit tests (tests/src/math/internal/types/big_int and dyadic_float), so their APIs are real and exercised. DyadicFloat carries the extended-precision intermediate for correctly-rounded pow and for the trig Payne-Hanek reduction, which needs many bits of pi. Confirm the exact template signatures BigInt<Bits,Signed,WordType> and DyadicFloat<Bits> by reading the headers.

float128 and int128 are portability wrappers: they use the compiler __int128 and __float128 where available and fall back to BigInt-based emulation otherwise (verify the fallback path). ctz.hpp and bits.hpp provide constexpr count-trailing-zeros and popcount used by BigInt normalization. These types are the bridge between the Type System page and the correctly-rounded results described on the Power and Exponential pages.

pages.title: Testing and Benchmarks
pages.purpose: Overview the quality-assurance machinery: 129 test files plus benchmark and fuzzing trees, all wired through CMake helpers. Summarize the layered testing strategy as follows. Fast unit tests under tests/src/ (mirroring the public family layout) run on every CI. A conformance suite under tests/conformance/ checks fenv exceptions, rounding-mode behavior, ULP across all modes, and include policy. A rigorous suite under tests/rigorous/ validates against external oracles (MPFR, CORE-MATH, cross-libm) with results captured in tests/rigorous/oracle_logs/. A proofs tree under tests/proofs/ ties implementation constants to Gappa and Sollya derivations with a freshness check. Shared infrastructure under tests/shared/ holds oracle harnesses and utils such as the ULP suite, fp_matcher, fenv_fixture, and conformance_suite. Separately summarize the Google Benchmark performance harness under benchmarks/ and the libFuzzer differential-fuzzing harness under fuzzing/. State the policy that tests land in the same change as the implementation. Link to the three child pages: Testing Framework, Benchmark System, and Fuzz Testing.

page_notes.content: Test policy: tests land in the same change as the implementation and must cover ULP, edge cases, fenv flags, and worst-case inputs. The rigorous and oracle layer requires MPFR and GMP and optionally CORE-MATH (found via cmake/config/FindMpfrGmp.cmake and FindCoreMath.cmake) and is gated behind a build option, so it does not run in the default consumer build.

tests/ structure: src/ (unit tests, the bulk of the 129 files), conformance/, proofs/, rigorous/, shared/ (oracle/ and utils/), plus ccmath_test_main.cpp and CMakeLists.txt. The suite uses GoogleTest. The shared utils worth naming: ulp_suite.hpp, ulp_distance.hpp, fp_matcher.hpp, fenv_fixture.hpp, conformance_suite.hpp, worst_case_samples.hpp, pow_path_dispatch.hpp, and campaign_metadata.hpp.

pages.title: Testing Framework
pages.purpose: Explain how the GoogleTest-based suite is organized and how to add a test. Cover the CMake helper ccmath_add_test (cmake/helpers/CcmTestHelpers.cmake) and the registry (cmake/config/TestModuleRegistry.cmake, TestCtestTargets.cmake) that turn a source file into a CTest target, plus the shared entry point tests/ccmath_test_main.cpp. Enumerate the unit-test categories mirroring the public API under tests/src/math/ (basic, compare, expo, fmanip, nearest, power, trig, misc) and tests/src/math/ext/ (28 extension tests), plus the internal-type tests under tests/src/math/internal/ (big_int, dyadic_float, normalized_float, math_support, SIMD). Describe the conformance suite (tests/conformance/): fenv_exception_test, fenv_probe_test, fma_rounding_test, fma_software_fenv_test, include_policy_test, pow_errno_fenv_test, pow_path_config_test, powl_platform_test, rounding_conformance_test, ulp_all_modes_test, ulp_distance_test, and worst_case_ulp_test. Explain the ULP-accuracy framework in tests/shared/utils/ (ulp_suite.hpp, ulp_distance.hpp, fp_matcher.hpp) and how worst-case inputs (worst_case_samples.hpp) and math samples (math_samples.hpp) feed it. Note the campaign concept (campaign_metadata.hpp, pow_path_dispatch.hpp) that selects which code path a given test actually measures.

page_notes.content: A pitfall worth documenting prominently: a test can silently measure the system libm instead of the ccmath kernel depending on the campaign and path. The simple and public_default campaigns hit libm via __builtin_*, while the generic_runtime campaign hits the ccmath kernel. pow_path_dispatch.hpp and campaign_metadata.hpp control this. Always confirm which path a new numeric test exercises. Cross-link to the Power Functions page note about this.

MSVC test-portability notes: watch for C4127 (conditional expression is constant) and use CCM_NEVER_INLINE where needed. The ULP policy and the oracle exceptional-value policy are documented. Verify exact ULP thresholds against docs/ULP_STATUS.md rather than hardcoding numbers here. include_policy_test.cpp enforces that public headers never include <cmath>.

pages.title: Benchmark System
pages.purpose: Document the performance-measurement harness under benchmarks/. Explain the Google Benchmark integration and the add_benchmark CMake helper (cmake/helpers/CcmBenchHelpers.cmake, registered via cmake/config/BenchmarkModuleRegistry.cmake) gated behind the build-benchmarks option. Describe the shared harness in benchmarks/shared/: harness.hpp (the benchmark framework glue), inputs.hpp (randomized and representative input generation), paths.hpp (selecting which implementation path to benchmark, mirroring the three-path model so builtin, generic, and runtime or SIMD can be compared), and register.hpp (target registration). Enumerate the current benchmark targets under benchmarks/src/math/: basic/abs.bench.cpp, basic/fdim.bench.cpp, basic/fma.bench.cpp, nearest/trunc.bench.cpp, and power/sqrt.bench.cpp. Note that benchmarks compare CCMath against std:: and libm baselines and across paths, and mention any visualization tooling (plot scripts) if present. Cross-link to the tools/asmlab/ directory, which provides deeper assembly, accuracy, and throughput analysis beyond the Google Benchmark numbers.

page_notes.content: Confirmed benchmark targets: abs, fdim, and fma (basic), trunc (nearest), and sqrt (power). Possible visualization scripts such as plot.py or run_benchmark_and_plot are unverified, so confirm they exist under benchmarks/ before documenting them (they may live in tools/asmlab/scripts/ instead). benchmarks/shared/paths.hpp is what makes the harness useful: it lets a benchmark target a specific dispatch path, so builtin, generic, and runtime numbers are directly comparable.

tools/asmlab/ is a separate analysis lab (accuracy/, bench/, golden/, harness/, knowledge/, refdata/, registry/, scripts/, docker/) for assembly inspection and golden-output regression. By project convention, asmlab shipped scripts and its public README stay command-focused and free of internal-tooling references. Keep asmlab documentation factual and command-oriented.

pages.title: Fuzz Testing
pages.purpose: Document the libFuzzer-based differential-fuzzing infrastructure under fuzzing/. Explain the goal: feed random bit patterns as floating-point inputs into ccmath functions and compare against a reference (the system libm or an oracle), flagging ULP regressions, wrong special-case handling, or divergent fenv or errno behavior. Cover the shared harness in fuzzing/shared/: entry.hpp (the LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput entry points), input.hpp (decoding raw bytes into typed FP inputs), fuzz_calls.hpp (the per-function call wrappers), and harness.hpp (the differential-comparison logic including a ulp_difference utility and a check_same_floating equality that treats NaN and sign-of-zero correctly). Enumerate the per-category fuzz targets under fuzzing/src/math/: basic_fuzz.cpp, compare_fuzz.cpp, expo_fuzz.cpp, fmanip_fuzz.cpp, misc_fuzz.cpp, nearest_fuzz.cpp, power_fuzz.cpp, and trig_fuzz.cpp. Describe the supporting pieces: the libFuzzer dictionary fuzzing/dicts/ccmath.dict, the seed generator fuzzing/scripts/generate_seeds.py, the CMake driver cmake/modules/CcmLibFuzzer.cmake and helper CcmFuzzHelpers.cmake, and the campaign and smoke runners fuzzing/cmake/run_fuzz_campaign.cmake and run_fuzz_smoke.cmake. Note that the smoke targets are wired into CTest for quick regression and the full campaign runs in the fuzz.yml GitHub workflow. Mention that fuzzing has already surfaced real kernel bugs.

page_notes.content: Eight fuzz targets are present (basic, compare, expo, fmanip, misc, nearest, power, trig). Differential fuzzing has found real kernel bugs, so treat it as an active bug-finding tool rather than a formality. The current git branch fix/fuzz-guard-ld80-powl-near-one is a direct result of fuzzing the ld80 powl path near 1.0.

check_same_floating and ulp_difference in fuzzing/shared/harness.hpp must handle NaN payloads and signed zero correctly to avoid false positives. fuzzing/cmake/run_fuzz_smoke.cmake feeds CTest (fast, every build) while run_fuzz_campaign.cmake is the long-running campaign used by .github/workflows/fuzz.yml. A libFuzzer-capable compiler (Clang) is required. See cmake/modules/CcmLibFuzzer.cmake for the gating.

pages.title: Glossary
pages.purpose: Define the codebase-specific vocabulary an onboarding engineer needs, each with code pointers. Terms to define. ccm: the public namespace for all functions. three-path dispatch: builtin versus generic versus runtime selection in the public header. generic kernel and _gen: portable constexpr implementation under internal/math/generic/. runtime kernel and _rt: runtime path, possibly SIMD, under internal/math/runtime/. FPBits: bit-level FP decomposition, internal/support/fp/fp_bits.hpp. FPRepImpl, FPType, and FPLayout: the format-parameterized representation hierarchy. BiasedExponent versus Exponent: stored-with-bias versus unbiased exponent. EFT or error-free transform: two-sum and two-product ops in internal/support/fp/ops/. ULP: unit in the last place, the accuracy metric, see docs/ULP_STATUS.md and tests/shared/utils/ulp_distance.hpp. correctly rounded or CR: the result equals the infinitely-precise value rounded once to the target format in the active mode. in-contract: meets the project's stated accuracy bound for that function, not necessarily CR. Ziv strategy: layered recompute at higher precision when the rounding is ambiguous. BigInt and DyadicFloat: extended-precision intermediates, internal/support/. Payne-Hanek: large-argument trig reduction. deterministic mode: CCMATH_ENABLE_DETERMINISTIC, forces the generic path for cross-hardware bit-identical results. CCM_HAS_BUILTIN and CCM_HAS_CONST_BUILTIN: builtin-availability macros, internal/predef/. ccm::intrin: the live SIMD layer. ccm::pp: the standalone C++26 std::simd port under runtime/pp/, not yet wired. SVML: the Intel Short Vector Math Library, detected in cmake/features/simd/. campaign or path dispatch: which implementation a test actually measures, tests/shared/utils/pow_path_dispatch.hpp. oracle: a high-precision reference such as MPFR or CORE-MATH used in tests/shared/oracle/. fenv and errno semantics: IEEE exception flags and C error reporting honored per <cmath>.

page_notes.content: Keep each glossary entry to a one-or-two-sentence definition plus a concrete file pointer so it doubles as a navigation index. The CR-versus-in-contract distinction is the most important pair to get right for this codebase and should be crisp. CR means rounded once correctly in the active mode, while in-contract means within the documented ULP bound (see docs/ULP_STATUS.md and docs/ROUNDING_STATUS.md for which functions achieve which).

Keep all pointers to tracked files only, since the wiki is committed. Do not reference local-only or untracked tooling paths that the project keeps out of version control. Verify the ccm::pp and ccm::intrin naming against include/ccmath/internal/math/runtime/ before finalizing.
